question: Design a base class `Publication` with private attributes `title` (string) and `price` (double). Implement methods to set and display these. From `Publication`, derive `Book` (adds private attribute `author` - string) and `Magazine` (adds private attribute `issueNumber` - int). Demonstrate creating objects of `Book` and `Magazine` and displaying their complete details.

test_cases.expected:
--- Book Details (Partial) ---
Publication Info:
  Title is: Programming in C++
  Cost is: $55.99
  Author is: Robert Lafore

--- Magazine Details (Partial) ---
Publication Info:
  Title is: Linux Journal
  Cost is: $8.75
  Issue Number is: 305

--- Demonstrating full display by calling separately ---
Book:
Publication Info:
  Title is: Programming in C++
  Cost is: $55.99
  Author is: Robert Lafore
Magazine:
Publication Info:
  Title is: Linux Journal
  Cost is: $8.75
  Issue Number is: 305

errors: Readability: Inconsistent and generic naming conventions for attributes (e.g., `title_val`, `writer_name`) and methods (e.g., `set_details_pub`, `show_book_info`).

Readability: Derived classes' display methods (`show_book_info`, `show_magazine_data`) do not call the base class's display method (`show_details_pub`). This forces the user to manually call multiple display methods to get complete details, making the code less intuitive and more error-prone.
Readability: Excessive and redundant use of `this->` keyword, which can clutter code without adding significant clarity.

Efficiency: String parameters in setter methods are passed by value (`std::string title`, `std::string author`) instead of by constant reference (`const std::string&`). This causes unnecessary string copying, which can be inefficient for large strings.
Readability: Lack of constructors means objects must be initialized via multiple setter calls, which can be less concise and readable than using a constructor for initial setup.
